comments.text: I would guess that the natural density of $S$ is $1$. Heuristic: write $N_m=m!+1$. For every prime $q\le m$, we have $m!\equiv 0 \pmod q$, hence $N_m\equiv 1 \pmod q$, so every prime divisor of $N_m$ is $>m$. The condition $m\notin S$ means that every prime divisor $p$ of $N_m$ satisfies $p\equiv 1 \pmod m$. Thus the exceptional set consists of those $m$ for which all prime factors of $m!+1$ lie in the single residue class $1 \pmod m$. Now treat $N_m$ as having “random-looking” prime divisors, subject only to the local restrictions above. Since $\log\log(m!+1)\sim \log m$, one expects $N_m$ to have about $\log m$ distinct prime factors. For a random large prime not dividing $m$, the chance of lying in the class $1 \pmod m$ should be about $1/\varphi(m)$. So the chance that all prime factors of $N_m$ lie in that one class should be roughly $$(1/\varphi(m))^{\log m},$$ which tends to $0$ extremely fast. This suggests that the number of exceptional $m\le x$ should be $o(x)$, and therefore $$|S\cap [1,x]|/x \to 1.$$ So my guess for the first question is that the limit exists and equals $1$. This also seems more plausible than a limit near $1/2$: the condition defining the complement of $S$ is much more rigid than merely asking for one prime factor in a prescribed congruence class. One is requiring that every prime factor of $m!+1$ fall into the same residue class $1 \pmod m$, and heuristically that should be very rare. References: Hardy and Subbarao, *On Euclid’s algorithm and the theory of infinitely many primes*, Colloq. Math. 44 (1981), 47–59. Subbarao, *Some remarks on Euclid’s number*, in *Number Theory and its Applications* (Ankara, 1996), Lecture Notes in Pure and Appl. Math. 204, Dekker, 1999, 281–290. Guy and Selfridge, unstarred problem on Erdős–Selfridge–Hardy numbers / Pillai primes, as listed on Erdős Problems.

Yes, I agree the density should be $1$, and think this is also the belief of Hardy and Subbarao (and Erdős), although they phrase it in a confusing manner (which is partially why I quoted the paragraph in full).
in the second quote, "monotonoically" is written rather than "monotonoically". (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Yeah, just a small typo. It should have been "monotonoically" of course. (Sorry, couldn't resist)
If the density of EHS numbers is proven to be one, then we would also prove the folklore conjecture that $n!+1$ is almost always composite.
